title: Behold, a New Kind of Democracy in Full Bloom!
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The Tamil below is a source-checked reading copy; the frozen source chapter remains unchanged and authoritative. The page images restore the OCR-omitted opening `உடன்பிறப்பே,` and confirm that the damaged Madurai phrase reads `இடைத் தேர்தல்`. Clearly mechanical OCR character errors and word breaks are repaired without changing the source’s allegations or their attribution. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement.
paragraphs: Because the BJP candidate withdrew her nomination in the Tirunelveli mayoral election, the AIADMK candidate has apparently been elected unopposed. Is this the situation only there? The AIADMK has reportedly captured all four municipal chairperson posts as well. This is today’s eight-column headline in *Dina Thanthi*. But did they win these victories by securing the people’s votes?

Realising that the ruling AIADMK would not conduct these local-body by-elections properly, not only the DMK but most opposition parties in Tamil Nadu announced beforehand that they would boycott them. The BJP and the Communist parties, however, declared that they would contest. And what suffering has the ruling party inflicted upon their candidates?
When by-elections are to be held for so many local bodies, the Election Commission must announce them after allowing every opposition party adequate time to select its candidates. Was that done?

Only on 28 August did the State Election Commission suddenly announce that the local-body by-elections would be held on 18 September, that nominations would open that very day, and that the final date for filing them would be 4 September 2014.

The amusing part is that the Election Commission had already announced these same local-body elections once before, on 6 August 2014, and then, in an absurd performance, withdrew its own election announcement on the very day it issued it.

After first announcing the election and cancelling that announcement, the Election Commission announced it again twenty days later. Did it change the previously announced polling date of 18 September? No. The second announcement, issued twenty days later, said the election would be held on exactly the same date that had been announced and cancelled on 6 August.

The day before the election announcement, the chairperson of Cuddalore Municipality resigned following disputes with an AIADMK Minister and the Municipal Commissioner. The very next day after the resignation, a by-election was announced for Cuddalore Municipality. Even *Dinamalar* noted that never in the history of Tamil Nadu’s local elections had a by-election been announced the day after an office became vacant.
Only after preparing all the AIADMK candidates did the State Election Commission announce the election date, giving no time to the other opposition parties.

After all this, when the BJP and Communist parties nevertheless came forward to contest, did the ruling party at least agree to conduct these elections democratically? Shall I give you a few examples of how the AIADMK behaved in the local-body by-elections?

When BJP and Communist candidates arrived to file nominations for the Pudukkottai Municipal Chairperson’s post, AIADMK members prevented them from even entering. Those parties held a protest against this. They were not permitted to file their nominations at all, and the ruling-party candidate was declared elected.

In the Tirunelveli mayoral election, the BJP candidate suddenly withdrew, we are told, and the AIADMK candidate was elected unopposed! But do you know what happened there? Ten people, including the BJP candidate, had filed nominations. Apart from those of the BJP and AIADMK candidates, every nomination was rejected. Yesterday, the last date for withdrawal, the BJP candidate withdrew her nomination.
But the Tamil Nadu BJP president said in an interview:

“In many places, BJP candidates are being threatened and assaulted so that they will not contest. When complaints are made to the police, they refuse even to receive them. In these circumstances, Vellaiyammal, the BJP mayoral candidate in Tirunelveli, had been threatened for the past three days and had kept the party informed. She was threatened, her signature obtained, and her nomination made to be withdrawn. She was also told that she would be admitted into the AIADMK.

“A disgraceful condition has now arisen in Tamil Nadu in which opposition parties are not allowed to contest elections. In many places where BJP members filed nominations, the notice board initially states that their nominations have been accepted. Officials later announce that they have been rejected. How is this fair in any way? The ruling party is engaging in lawlessness by threatening and attacking candidates and forcing them to withdraw their nominations. It is acting in this manner because it wants opposition parties not to win and itself to win without a contest.”

Condemning irregularities in the local-body by-elections, members of the Communist parties held a siege protest before the State Election Commission office in Koyambedu. More than one hundred Communist party members were arrested by the police.

Nor was Tirunelveli the only incident. The nomination of the CPI(M) candidate for Ward 2 of the Chetpet Panchayat Union in Tiruvannamalai district was wrongly rejected. The party’s State Secretary, Comrade G. Ramakrishnan, lodged a complaint and pressed for action.

After an inquiry, Tamil Nadu State Election Commission Secretary Jyothi Nirmala issued a statement. It said that the District Collector’s detailed report on the complaint had been examined; that when nominations for the Ward 2 by-election in the Chetpet Panchayat Union were scrutinised on the fifth, the Returning Officer and Block Development Officer had failed to follow the election rules properly; and that orders had therefore been issued to suspend them immediately and conduct an inquiry. The election itself was also cancelled.

It is said that the Election Commission was compelled to take proper action in this instance only because Communist party members had photographed the candidate list posted at the site and produced it as evidence that could not be denied!
By-elections were due in Wards 7 and 33 of Tambaram Municipality. The BJP nominations were rejected, and the AIADMK candidates who had filed nominations were declared elected unopposed.

In Ward 2 of Pallavaram Municipality, six people, including the BJP candidate, filed nominations. Every nomination apart from that of the ruling-party candidate was made to be withdrawn, and the AIADMK candidate was declared elected unopposed.

In Ward 33 of Avadi Municipality, eleven people, including a BJP candidate, filed nominations. Two nominations were rejected. On 8 September 2014, officials said that the remaining eight candidates, including the BJP candidate, had withdrawn, and the ruling-party candidate was declared elected. It was also claimed that the BJP candidate and the substitute candidate had joined the AIADMK. The BJP held a protest and road blockade in condemnation. The police arrested the protesters and released them at night.

A by-election was announced for Ward 166 in Alandur, and a BJP nomination was filed. But from yesterday morning, the BJP candidate was reportedly missing. Calls to his mobile phone went unanswered. More than twenty-five suspicious party members came to the Alandur zonal election office and waited carefully at the entrance to see whether their candidate arrived. At 1.35 p.m., however, the Election Officer said the BJP candidate had withdrawn his nomination. The BJP members argued: when they had been standing at the office entrance, how could he have entered and withdrawn it? The Election Officer reportedly replied that they did not know how he had come inside. It was alleged that the BJP candidate had been offered a large sum, promised admission to the AIADMK, and made to withdraw his nomination.

There was a by-election in Ward 85 of the Madurai Corporation. The BJP candidate filed a nomination and conducted an intensive campaign. But the election official announced that both the BJP candidate and the substitute BJP candidate had withdrawn their nominations and that the AIADMK candidate had therefore won unopposed.

Had either BJP candidate signed a withdrawal? Certainly not. Two unidentified persons came to the Madurai Corporation office claiming to be the proposers of those nominations and said they were withdrawing them. On that basis, the official declared the nominations withdrawn. In fact, the person who had proposed the BJP candidate was campaigning with the candidate at that very time. Thus unfolded the drama in which an official trusted signatures placed by two unidentified persons using the proposers’ names and declared the AIADMK candidate elected unopposed.

The BJP candidate contesting for the Ramanathapuram Municipal Chairperson’s post complained to the Collector and Superintendent of Police that AIADMK members were threatening him. According to his complaint, they came to him and said: “We ourselves have forged your signature and submitted a letter saying that you are withdrawing from the contest. You can no longer contest.”
The family of Prabhakaran, the BJP candidate who contested a panchayat seat in Melmalayanur, Villupuram district, has reportedly said that he is missing.

It was precisely because we expected such trickery, wrongdoing and lawlessness that the Dravida Munnetra Kazhagam boycotted these local-body by-elections. Several other parties in Tamil Nadu also stayed away. Yet when even the candidates of the one or two parties that did contest are threatened, subdued, intimidated and made to withdraw by the AIADMK, could there be any greater disgrace for a ruling party?
With affection,
M.K.
10 September 2014
